title: Sieving and storing finished compost
subtitle: Getting a consistent, fine-grade product and keeping it usable until the growing season
excerpt: A 10-12 mm riddle sieve separates finished compost into a fine material suitable for seed sowing and potting on, and coarser oversize material that goes back into the active heap. Finished compost stored wet in a sealed container loses nitrogen; stored dry it is stable for 6-12 months.
sourceNotes: RHS guidance on compost maturity and sieving. Charles Dowding no-dig method notes on compost grading and use. Garden Organic guidance on storing and applying compost.

content.text: before sieving avoids the problem of sieving too early. If the heap still smells of ammonia or shows warm patches, it is not finished: leave it for another 2-4 weeks. A simple maturity test: fill a small pot with the compost, sow a few cress seeds, and observe after 5 days. Mature compost produces normal, straight seedlings. Immature compost may inhibit germination or produce distorted, stunted seedlings.
6 mm mesh: very fine grade suitable for seed sowing compost and top-dressing lawns. Slow to sieve; best for small quantities.
10 mm mesh: standard grade for potting on, mulching established plants, and adding to raised beds. Most practical balance of speed and quality.
15-20 mm mesh: coarse grade for path mulching or soil structure improvement where fine grade is not needed.

Material that does not pass through the sieve is not wasted. Return it to the active compost heap as a starter culture: it already contains microorganisms and will accelerate decomposition of the new material. Do not store oversize material separately; it dries out and loses its biological activity.

Finished compost should be stored at moderate moisture: moist enough to keep microorganisms alive but not waterlogged, which causes nitrogen loss through denitrification. The ideal is covered but not sealed: a tarpaulin over a heap, or a lidded but not airtight container. Fully sealed storage of wet compost causes anaerobic conditions and loss of ammonia. If compost will be used within 4-6 weeks, storage conditions matter less; for long-term storage of 6+ months, keep it covered and at garden-ambient moisture.
